ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb meat food product class; no specific grilled ribloin cutlet entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers sheep meat broadly; no ribloin-specific grilled cutlet code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for grilled lamb ribloin cutlet; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one grilled lamb ribloin cutlet with bone, approximately 120 g, yielding roughly 80–90 g of edible lean and fat meat after accounting for bone weight.

nutrient_source.title: Grilled Lamb Ribloin Cutlet — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06050003 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros and micronutrients are taken as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are not individually reported by HPB for this entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb rib chop (NDB 17059) is used as a secondary reference to estimate saturated fat at approximately 7.4 g/100 g and to provide values for MUFA (~7.8 g/100 g), PUFA (~1.3 g/100 g), and trans fat (~0.4 g/100 g).

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lamb ribloin cutlet contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (57 mg/100 g) with moderate potassium (272 mg/100 g); however, saturated fat at ~7.4 g/100 g exceeds the AHA 5 g/100 g threshold and may contribute to arterial stiffness over time.
hypertension.guidance: Grilled lamb ribloin cutlet is low in sodium and suitable for hypertension management when consumed in moderate portions (≤120 g), provided no high-sodium sauces are added and visible fat is trimmed before grilling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar produce no glycaemic impact; high protein (27.2 g/100 g) supports satiety, though saturated fat at ~7.4 g/100 g may worsen insulin resistance if consumed in excess.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled lamb ribloin cutlet is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its zero carbohydrate content and high protein density; limit portion to 100–120 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and avoid sugary marinades or glazes per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~7.4 g/100 g and ruminant trans fat at ~0.4 g/100 g are both pro-atherogenic; cholesterol at 91 mg/100 g is moderate; however, sodium is low and the food is unprocessed.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit grilled lamb ribloin cutlet to occasional consumption (≤1–2 times per week), trim all visible fat before grilling, and balance with omega-3-rich foods such as oily fish to offset the saturated and trans fat burden per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid content in red meat drives RA inflammatory pathways; saturated fat at ~7.4 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; ruminant trans fat (~0.4 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled lamb ribloin cutlet is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat, ruminant trans fat, and arachidonic acid content; substitute with oily fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids to support anti-inflammatory dietary management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled lamb ribloin cutlet is a bone-in red meat dish prepared from the rib and loin section of a young sheep (lamb), grilled at high heat until cooked through with a slightly charred exterior. The cut retains both internal marbling and external fat, yielding a rich, savoury flavour with a tender yet slightly firm texture. In Singapore, this preparation is commonly found in mamak stalls and Indian-Muslim restaurants, where it is served as a main protein alongside rice, roti, or flatbreads. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 278 kcal, 27.2 g protein, 18.9 g total fat (approximately 7.4 g saturated, 0.4 g ruminant trans fat), 0 g carbohydrate, 57 mg sodium, 272 mg potassium, 9 mg calcium, 2.16 mg iron, and 91 mg cholesterol. The zero carbohydrate content makes it glycaemically inert and suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. The high protein density supports muscle maintenance and satiety. Clinically, the low sodium content is favourable for hypertension, but the saturated fat and ruminant trans fat content warrants moderation for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. The food is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is grilled lamb chop suitable for diabetes?
How many calories in a grilled lamb cutlet?
Is lamb ribloin high in protein?
Is grilled lamb good for high blood pressure?
How much saturated fat in lamb ribloin?
Can I eat lamb chop if I have heart disease?
What is the sodium content of grilled lamb?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06050003, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb rib chop (NDB 17059) as a secondary approved source. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a grilled bone-in lamb ribloin cut.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo; this is naturally zero as lamb ribloin cutlet is a pure animal protein and fat product containing no carbohydrate-bearing ingredients. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal-derived food with no plant fibre. Saturated_fat_g (~7.4 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (~7.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (~1.3 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (~0.4 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17059 (lamb, domestic, rib, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, cooked, broiled) as HPB does not individually report fatty acid fractions for this entry. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because HPB Singapore Foodo does not report these values for this specific entry (cr_id F06050003), and while USDA SR Legacy NDB 17059 provides some analogous values, they are not confirmed for the Singapore HPB preparation context and are therefore withheld as sentinel values pending a dedicated micronutrient review: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Source consulted and confirmed unavailable for this preparation: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. Glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Primary cuisine tagged as mamak per input cultural metadata.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, documented), sodium_mg 57.0, saturated_fat_g 7.4 (estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 17059, documented), sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero, documented). All four required secondary fields present and numeric.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) per critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source present and documented. Food contains 0.0 g carbohydrate; GI not applicable.
